questions.question: What makes VLA (vision-language-action) models harder than VQA-style VLMs?
options: Robots can't carry GPUs

Continuous high-dimensional action spaces (10 dims at 30 Hz = 33ms per action), scarce robot data (~1M Open X-Embodiment trajectories vs 5B+ web pairs), and safety — a wrong action damages hardware or humans
Cameras produce lower-quality images
Action data cannot be tokenized at all
questions.explanation: Same VLM architecture, but the output is actions (joint torques, end-effector poses) instead of text — and the control-frequency budget and data scarcity reshape every design choice.

questions.question: What was RT-2's action-tokenization trick, and why co-fine-tune on web data?
options: It output raw motor voltages as floats

Discretize each normalized joint target into 256 bins mapped to vocabulary IDs (a 10-DOF action = 10 tokens), co-fine-tuned with web image-text so general knowledge transfers — e.g., following "move towards the fast-moving object" never seen in robot data
It generated Python scripts for the robot
Actions were predicted by a separate CNN
questions.explanation: RT-2 (July 2023) proved web-scale vision-language knowledge transfers to control. The cost: autoregressive VLM decode limited inference to 3-5 Hz.

questions.question: What is OpenVLA, and what are its specs?
options: A closed Google API for robots

The open-weights RT-2 equivalent: 7B Llama backbone, DINOv2 + SigLIP dual vision encoder, 256-bin action tokens, trained on Open X-Embodiment (970k trajectories, 22 robots), with LoRA support — 4-5 Hz on an A100
A simulator with no real-robot support
A 70B model requiring a datacenter
questions.explanation: Fast enough for slow manipulation, not high-frequency control. Open X-Embodiment is the field's shared corpus — the RT-X training data pooled across labs and robot embodiments.

questions.question: How does the FAST tokenizer speed up action decoding?
options: By using a faster GPU kernel

Discrete-bin tokens are inefficient because actions cluster in a small region of bin-space — FAST compresses action sequences via DCT and quantizes the coefficients, turning a 30-step trajectory of 300 bin-tokens into ~10 tokens for a 3-5x speedup
By dropping every other action
By caching previous trajectories
questions.explanation: Frequency-domain compression (like JPEG for trajectories) exploits the smoothness of real robot motion — fewer tokens to decode autoregressively, no quality loss.

questions.question: How do π0 and GR00T N1 depart from discrete action tokens?
options: They abandon vision input entirely

π0 replaces tokens with a flow-matching action expert producing continuous actions; GR00T N1 uses a dual-system design — a deliberate System 2 reasoner plus a fast System 1 controller — for humanoid robots at scale
They use reinforcement learning only
They output natural-language instructions to a human
questions.explanation: Continuous flow-matching sidesteps quantization error and autoregressive latency for smooth high-frequency control; the dual-system split mirrors the think-slow/act-fast division the control loop demands.
